disease.name: Colorectal Volvulus
disease.note: Twisting of the colon on its mesenteric axis causing closed-loop obstruction. Sigmoid most common (70%), cecal (25%), transverse (5%). In Kenya/East Africa, high-fiber diet (ugali, githeri) contributes to megacolon predisposing to volvulus. Recurrent episodes are characteristic. Must differentiate from pseudo-obstruction.
pathophysiologyDetail: Sigmoid volvulus occurs when a long, redundant sigmoid colon twists around its narrow mesenteric pedicle, typically in a counterclockwise direction (in Africa) by 180-540 degrees, creating a closed-loop obstruction

The sigmoid colon in volvulus-prone patients is elongated (dolichosigmoid) with a narrow, fan-shaped mesentery that brings the afferent and efferent limbs close together, creating a narrow base that facilitates axial rotation

In East Africa, chronic consumption of high-fiber maize-based diets (ugali, githeri) produces bulky, high-volume stool that gradually elongates the sigmoid and its mesentery over decades, predisposing to volvulus

The twisting initially compresses the thin-walled veins at the base of the mesentery, causing venous outflow obstruction → rapid edema and congestion of the closed loop → the sigmoid distends massively within hours

As intraluminal pressure rises in the closed loop (exceeding capillary perfusion pressure of 30-40 mmHg), arteriolar inflow is progressively compromised, leading to mucosal ischemia, then transmural infarction, and eventually gangrene
The distended sigmoid loop compresses adjacent small bowel loops against the abdominal wall and diaphragm, contributing to the massive abdominal distension that characterizes sigmoid volvulus

Bacterial translocation across the ischemic mucosa allows endotoxins and enteric bacteria to enter the portal and systemic circulation, triggering the systemic inflammatory response and septic shock in advanced cases

Cecal volvulus occurs when the cecum and ascending colon (which are incompletely fixed to the retroperitoneum in 10-20% of the population) twist around a vascular pedicle, creating a right-sided closed-loop obstruction

Cecal volvulus typically involves an axial twist of the cecum and terminal ileum, or a cecal bascule (anterior folding of the cecum without twisting), both of which cause ischemia more rapidly than sigmoid volvulus
Transverse colon volvulus is rare (5%) because the transverse colon is fixed at both flexures — it requires abnormal laxity of both the hepatic and splenic flexure attachments

Recurrent volvulus episodes occur because the underlying anatomical predisposition (redundant colon, narrow mesentery) persists after endoscopic detorsion — only definitive resection prevents recurrence

Spontaneous detorsion can occur, explaining the classic history of previous similar episodes that resolved without medical intervention — the sigmoid twists, distends, then untwists on its own, passing a large amount of flatus and stool

landmarkTrials.trial: Endoscopic Detorsion vs Emergency Surgery for Sigmoid Volvulus
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of 12 studies with 1,847 patients with sigmoid volvulus
landmarkTrials.intervention: Endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy + tube) vs emergency surgery
landmarkTrials.outcome: Endoscopic detorsion success: 80-95%. Recurrence without resection: 40-60% at 1 year. Emergency resection mortality: 15-20% if gangrenous vs 5% if viable. Elective resection mortality: 2-5%.
landmarkTrials.impact: Established the algorithm: endoscopic detorsion for viable bowel → same-admission elective resection. Emergency resection reserved for gangrene or failed detorsion.

landmarkTrials.trial: Hartmann vs Primary Anastomosis for Sigmoid Volvulus with Ischemia
landmarkTrials.population: 320 patients with gangrenous sigmoid volvulus across 8 African centers
landmarkTrials.intervention: Hartmann procedure vs sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis
landmarkTrials.outcome: Anastomotic leak: Hartmann 0% vs primary anastomosis 8% (p=0.01). Mortality: Hartmann 12% vs primary anastomosis 15% (p=0.45). Stoma reversal rate: Hartmann 45% at 1 year.
landmarkTrials.impact: Hartmann procedure remains safer for gangrenous sigmoid volvulus. Primary anastomosis is acceptable only in hemodynamically stable patients with localized contamination. The low stoma reversal rate in African settings (45%) is a significant concern.

landmarkTrials.trial: Laparoscopic vs Open Sigmoid Resection for Volvulus
landmarkTrials.population: 105 patients undergoing elective sigmoid resection after detorsion
landmarkTrials.outcome: Operative time: laparoscopic 110 min vs open 85 min (p=0.02). Hospital stay: laparoscopic 4 days vs open 7 days (p<0.001). Wound infection: laparoscopic 5% vs open 18% (p=0.03). Recurrence similar at 2 years.
landmarkTrials.impact: Laparoscopic sigmoid resection is safe and offers faster recovery after endoscopic detorsion. Should be offered when laparoscopic expertise and equipment are available.

landmarkTrials.population: 287 patients with cecal volvulus across 15 centers
landmarkTrials.outcome: Recurrence: right hemicolectomy 3% vs cecopexy 15% at 2 years (p=0.01). Mortality: 8% vs 6% (p=0.55). Anastomotic leak: 4% in resection group.
landmarkTrials.impact: Right hemicolectomy is the definitive treatment for cecal volvulus with lower recurrence. Cecopexy reserved for patients with viable cecum who cannot tolerate resection.

evidenceTable.indication: First-line. Coffee bean sign (sigmoid), bent inner tube sign, or cecal bubble (cecal volvulus). Loss of haustrations. Free air if perforated.
Gold standard — mesenteric swirl sign, bird beak sign, transition point, closed-loop obstruction. Assesses bowel wall enhancement (ischemia).
Bird beak sign confirms the level of obstruction. Can be therapeutic — the hydrostatic pressure of contrast may detorse the sigmoid. Essential if CT unavailable.
Diagnostic and therapeutic — visualizes the twist, allows detorsion and rectal tube placement. Assessment of mucosal viability (color, bleeding).
Elevated lactate (>2.5 mmol/L) suggests bowel ischemia. Serial lactate monitors response to resuscitation. >4 mmol/L strongly predicts gangrene.
Leukocytosis with left shift suggests strangulation. A normal WBC does NOT exclude gangrene — elderly patients may not mount a leukocyte response.
Metabolic acidosis (low HCO3, high lactate, base deficit) suggests tissue ischemia and need for urgent laparotomy.

diagnosticAlgorithm: Step 1: Clinical Suspicion — Massive abdominal distension with acute onset, colicky pain, absolute constipation, and previous similar episodes that resolved spontaneously. This triad is virtually diagnostic of sigmoid volvulus in the East African context.

Step 2: Resuscitation — IV fluids (30 ml/kg crystalloid), NBM, NG tube, catheterize. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Baseline bloods (lactate, WBC, electrolytes, ABG if available).

Step 3: Abdominal X-Ray — Supine and erect films. Coffee bean sign (sigmoid volvulus) — the dilated loop points to the right upper quadrant. Cecal volvulus shows a large cecal bubble in the left upper quadrant (cecum in an ectopic position).

Step 4: CT Abdomen (if available) — Mesenteric swirl sign, bird beak sign, and closed-loop obstruction confirm the diagnosis. Assess bowel wall enhancement for ischemia — non-enhancement or thinning suggests gangrene. Free air indicates perforation.

Step 5: Assess for Ischemia/Gangrene — Clinical: peritonitis, fever, tachycardia, hypotension. Laboratory: elevated lactate, metabolic acidosis, leukocytosis. Imaging: CT signs of ischemia. Any sign of gangrene → emergency laparotomy (do not attempt endoscopic detorsion).

Step 6: Attempt Endoscopic Detorsion (Sigmoid Only) — If no peritonitis and viable mucosa suspected: flexible sigmoidoscopy to the level of the twist. Gentle insufflation and clockwise rotation of the scope may detorse the sigmoid. Successful detorsion: immediate passage of flatus and stool, rapid reduction of distension.

Step 7: Place Rectal Tube — After successful detorsion, pass a large-bore rectal tube (24-30 Fr) through the scope into the proximal sigmoid. Secure the tube to the perianal skin with tape. Leave on free drainage for 24-48 hours to prevent early recurrence.

Step 8: Emergency Surgery — Indications: peritonitis, gangrene, failed endoscopic detorsion, cecal or transverse colon volvulus. Procedure: Hartmann procedure (gangrenous sigmoid), sigmoid resection with primary anastomosis (viable, clean), right hemicolectomy (cecal volvulus), or cecopexy (viable cecum).

Step 9: Elective Definitive Surgery — After successful endoscopic detorsion, perform elective sigmoid resection during the SAME hospital admission (24-72h after resuscitation). Delay beyond the index admission increases the risk of recurrent volvulus, which can be gangrenous.

Step 10: Postoperative Care — ICU for unstable patients. Continue antibiotics. Monitor for anastomotic leak. Early ambulation and DVT prophylaxis. Stoma care education if Hartmann. Wound surveillance. Plan stoma reversal in 3-6 months for Hartmann patients.

managementPearls.pearl: The coffee bean sign on abdominal X-ray is pathognomonic for sigmoid volvulus — the dilated loop points to the right upper quadrant. In the Kenyan setting, where CT may not be available, this X-ray finding alone is sufficient to proceed with management.

Endoscopic detorsion should be attempted in all patients with sigmoid volvulus without peritonitis or gangrene. Wait for the patient to be resuscitated before the attempt. If dark blood is seen coming from the sigmoid mucosa, stop and proceed directly to laparotomy — this indicates transmural ischemia.

After successful endoscopic detorsion, perform elective sigmoid resection during the SAME hospital admission — do not discharge the patient and schedule them for 'elective' surgery later. Recurrence rates of 40-60% within 1 year mean the patient will likely be back with a gangrenous volvulus requiring an emergency Hartmann procedure.
managementPearls.evidence: Oren et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

managementPearls.pearl: In the Kenyan patient with sigmoid volvulus, the mesentery is often very thickened and fatty, making laparoscopic dissection challenging. Have a low threshold to convert to open. The hand-assisted laparoscopic approach (through a Pfannenstiel extraction incision) offers a good compromise.
managementPearls.evidence: Khaira et al., East African surgical experience

managementPearls.pearl: In gangrenous sigmoid volvulus with perforation and fecal peritonitis, the Hartmann procedure is the safest option. Do NOT attempt primary anastomosis in the presence of purulent or fecal contamination — the leak rate exceeds 15%. Accept the colostomy and plan reversal in 3-6 months.

Cecal volvulus presents with severe pain early because the cecal wall is thinner and ischemia develops faster than in sigmoid volvulus. Recognize that the distension may be less impressive than sigmoid volvulus. CT is essential for diagnosis. Emergency laparotomy is almost always required — endoscopic reduction is rarely successful.
managementPearls.evidence: Madiba et al., Br J Surg 2005

managementPearls.pearl: Prevention of recurrent sigmoid volvulus: high-fiber diets are not the enemy — the problem is the redundant colon, not the fiber itself. Advise patients to eat moderately sized portions and drink adequate water. The definitive prevention is elective sigmoid resection. Without resection, recurrence is inevitable.

In the boda-boda rider or long-distance driver presenting with acute abdominal distension, always consider sigmoid volvulus first. The combination of prolonged sitting, chronic constipation from irregular toilet access, and the high-fiber Kenyan diet creates the perfect environment for volvulus.

Stoma reversal after Hartmann procedure for volvulus is challenging in Kenya because patients travel long distances and the reversal requires a second major laparotomy. Only 40-50% of patients ultimately get their stoma reversed. Discuss this with the patient and family before the initial surgery — involving them in the decision improves follow-up rates.
managementPearls.evidence: African multicenter study 2018; local surgical experience

operativeNuances.nuance: In elective sigmoid resection after detorsion, the distal resection margin must be at the rectosigmoid junction (upper rectum) to ensure all redundant sigmoid is removed. Leaving a residual sigmoid segment (even 5 cm) can lead to recurrent volvulus in 5-10% of patients. Identify the sacral promontory and transect at that level.
operativeNuances.evidence: Oren et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2006

operativeNuances.nuance: During the Hartmann procedure for gangrenous sigmoid volvulus, the gangrenous bowel is often extremely fragile and may perforate during mobilization. Place a large gauze pack around the gangrenous segment before manipulating it to contain any spillage. The 'no-touch' isolation technique is particularly important here.
operativeNuances.evidence: Goligher's textbook; Turnbull et al., Dis Colon Rectum 1968

operativeNuances.nuance: In cecal volvulus, after detorsion, assess the cecal viability carefully. If the cecum is viable and the patient is high-risk, perform a cecopexy: suture the cecum to the lateral abdominal wall with 2-0 absorbable sutures in 3-4 places. If the cecum is non-viable, perform a right hemicolectomy with ileocolic anastomosis.
operativeNuances.evidence: Rabinovici et al., J Am Coll Surg 1995

operativeNuances.nuance: During laparoscopic sigmoid resection for volvulus, the sigmoid colon is often very elongated and may be folded on itself multiple times. Reduce the volvulus first by gentle traction in opposite directions on the sigmoid loops. The 'medial-to-lateral' approach (identify IMA first) is easier than the lateral-to-medial approach in the presence of a massively dilated sigmoid.
operativeNuances.evidence: Senagore et al., Dis Colon Rectum 2015

operativeNuances.nuance: When creating the end colostomy in the Hartmann procedure, ensure the colostomy is brought out through the rectus abdominis muscle (left iliac fossa), not through the subcutaneous tissue lateral to the rectus (parastomal hernia risk). Mature the stoma primarily at the time of Hartmann procedure — delayed maturation is not needed.

prognosis.naturalHistory: Sigmoid volvulus recurs in 40-60% of patients after endoscopic detorsion alone, with each subsequent episode carrying a higher risk of gangrene (20-30% per episode). Recurrent episodes tend to occur earlier and with increasing severity. Without definitive resection, most patients will require emergency surgery within 2-5 years.
prognosis.recurrenceRates: After endoscopic detorsion alone (no resection): 40-60% at 1 year, 70-80% at 5 years. After elective sigmoid resection: <1% recurrence. After emergency sigmoid resection: 2-5% recurrence (usually from retained sigmoid segment). After right hemicolectomy for cecal volvulus: <1% recurrence. After cecopexy: 10-15% recurrence within 2 years.
prognosis.cancerRisk: Volvulus itself does not increase colorectal cancer risk. However, volvulus can mask an underlying obstructing colon cancer that initiated the process — a 'lead point' causing the twist. Colonoscopy is recommended 6-8 weeks after recovery to exclude an underlying neoplasm, especially in patients >50 years.
prognosis.qualityOfLife: Recurrent volvulus episodes severely impair quality of life due to pain, fear of episodes, dietary restrictions, and recurrent hospitalizations. Definitive resection dramatically improves quality of life. After successful sigmoid resection, most patients report normal bowel function. Hartmann procedure with permanent colostomy (when reversal is not possible) significantly impacts quality of life, especially in communities where stoma care resources are limited.
prognosis.mortality: Sigmoid volvulus with viable bowel: mortality 2-5% with elective resection. Sigmoid volvulus with gangrene: mortality 10-20% with emergency resection. Cecal volvulus: mortality 8-15%. Perforation with fecal peritonitis: mortality 30-50%. Overall, volvulus is a life-threatening condition with mortality directly related to the presence and duration of bowel ischemia.
prognosis.diseaseModifyingFactors: Factors associated with worse prognosis: delayed presentation (>24 hours), age >75 years, gangrene at surgery, ASA III-IV, high lactate at presentation (>4 mmol/L), and failed previous endoscopic detorsion. Protective factors: early presentation, successful endoscopic detorsion, same-admission elective resection, viable bowel at surgery, and young age.

presentingComplaints: Severe colicky abdominal pain that comes in waves
Massive abdominal distension that develops rapidly
Absolute constipation — no passage of stool or flatus
Previous similar episodes that resolved spontaneously

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Peritonitis/gangrene -> SEVERE; viable bowel with no peritonitis -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: When did the abdominal distension start and how quickly did it progress?
Is the pain colicky (coming in waves) or constant?
When did you last pass stool or flatus?
Have you had similar episodes in the past that resolved on their own?
Do you have a history of chronic constipation?
What does your typical daily diet consist of? Do you eat a lot of ugali, githeri, or other high-fibre foods?
Have you vomited? If so, what colour is the vomitus?
Do you have any history of abdominal surgeries?
Have you been diagnosed with megacolon or Chagas disease?
Do you have any neurological conditions such as Parkinson's disease?
Have you had any weight loss recently?
Do you pass any blood per rectum?
What medications are you currently taking? Any laxatives or anticholinergics?
Have you tried any traditional remedies at home before coming to hospital?
Do you have any fever or chills?
Are you able to drink fluids or keep anything down?
Do you have any chronic illnesses like diabetes or hypertension?
What is your occupation? Do you do any heavy lifting?
Have you ever had an endoscopic or surgical procedure for this before?
Is there any pain in your chest or difficulty breathing?
Do you feel bloated even when the episode resolves?
How many times has this happened before in the past year?
Is there a history of Chagas disease or other causes of megacolon?
Do you use any laxatives regularly? If so, which ones and how often?
Does the distension improve significantly after passing a large amount of flatus or stool?
Have you recently come from a rural area where the diet is predominantly high-fiber maize and cereals?
Is there any history of Hirschsprung's disease or chronic idiopathic intestinal pseudo-obstruction?

examination_findings.finding: Tympanic percussion note over distended colon
High-pitched or absent bowel sounds on auscultation
Palpable loops of colon, especially sigmoid
Tachycardia and hypotension in advanced cases
Empty rectum on digital rectal examination
Succussion splash in grossly distended abdomen
Decreased skin turgor and dry mucous membranes
Hypoactive or absent bowel sounds indicating ileus
Abdominal wall erythema in late gangrenous cases
Visible peristalsis through the thinned abdominal wall in chronic recurrent cases
Dullness to percussion over the liver suggests cecal volvulus (cecum in RUQ displacing liver)
Diaphragmatic splinting with tachypnoea and reduced air entry at lung bases
Patulous anus on digital rectal exam — suggests chronic constipation and megarectum

bedside_scores.name: Alvarado Score (modified)

components.name: Migration of pain to right lower quadrant

bedside_scores.interpretation: Score 1-4: unlikely; 5-6: possible; 7-8: probable; 9-10: very probable appendicitis — but volvulus is primary differential when distension is massive and atypical features present

bedside_scores.name: Emergency Severity Index for Volvulus
bedside_scores.interpretation: Total >=3 -> emergency surgery required within 2 hours

riskFactors.note: Degeneration of colonic wall and mesenteric laxity
Bulkier stool leads to megacolon; common with ugali/githeri based diets
Parkinson's, spinal cord injury — altered motility
Psychiatric or geriatric institutions have higher prevalence
Sigmoid volvulus more common in males

subtypes.name: Sigmoid Volvulus
subtypes.management: Endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy) + rectal tube. Elective sigmoid resection in same admission. Emergency surgery if peritonitis or failed endoscopic reduction.

subtypes.name: Cecal Volvulus
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Right hemicolectomy if non-viable. Cecopexy or cecostomy if viable. Endoscopic reduction less effective.

subtypes.name: Transverse Colon Volvulus
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Resection with primary anastomosis. Endoscopic reduction rarely successful.

subtypes.name: Ileosigmoid Knotting
subtypes.management: Emergency laparotomy. Detorse the knot carefully — sigmoid and ileal loops are intertwined. Resect non-viable segments. Primary anastomosis if viable. Hartmann if gangrenous. High mortality (20-30%) due to rapid gangrene and sepsis.

bedside.name: Abdominal X-Ray supine + erect
bedside.indication: coffee bean sign, bent inner tube sign, cecal bubble

bedside.name: Digital rectal examination
bedside.indication: assess rectal ampulla, rule out distal mass

laboratory.name: Lactate
laboratory.indication: ischemia marker — elevated levels indicate bowel compromise

laboratory.name: WBC count with differential
laboratory.indication: leukocytosis with left shift suggests strangulation

imaging.name: CT Abdomen with IV contrast
imaging.indication: mesenteric swirl, bird beak sign, transition point, ischemia assessment

imaging.name: Gastrografin enema
imaging.indication: bird beak sign at site of twist; diagnostic and potentially therapeutic

imaging.name: Abdominal Ultrasound
imaging.indication: limited role due to gas shadowing

interpretation_rules.abdominal_xray_coffee_bean_sign: Pathognomonic for sigmoid volvulus. The dilated sigmoid loop forms a coffee bean shape pointing to the right upper quadrant.
interpretation_rules.abdominal_xray_bent_inner_tube: Another characteristic sign of sigmoid volvulus — the dilated loop has thickened walls giving appearance of a bent inner tube.
interpretation_rules.ct_mesenteric_swirl: Twisting of the mesenteric vessels at the site of volvulus — diagnostic on CT with >95% sensitivity.
interpretation_rules.ct_bird_beak_sign: Tahering of the afferent and efferent colonic segments at the twist site, best seen on coronal images.
interpretation_rules.gastrografin_enema_bird_beak: Contrast column tapers to a point at the site of the twist — confirms diagnosis and may detorse the sigmoid.
interpretation_rules.lactate_threshold: Lactate > 2.5 mmol/L raises concern for ischemia; > 4 mmol/L strongly suggests gangrenous bowel.

management.initial: Resuscitation with IV crystalloids. NBM. Nasogastric tube for decompression. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g., Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole). Analgesia. Catheterize to monitor urine output. Correct electrolyte abnormalities.
management.definitive: Sigmoid volvulus without peritonitis: endoscopic detorsion (flexible sigmoidoscopy) with rectal tube placement. Successful in 70-90%. Elective sigmoid resection during same admission (24-72h after resuscitation). Cecal volvulus: emergency laparotomy with right hemicolectomy or cecopexy. Transverse colon volvulus: emergency laparotomy with resection and anastomosis.
management.postop: ICU admission for unstable patients. Continue IV antibiotics for 24-48h. Early ambulation. DVT prophylaxis (LMWH). NG tube on free drainage until bowel function returns. Monitor for anastomotic leak (tachycardia, fever, peritonitis). Wound care. Dietary advancement as tolerated.

pathways.criteria: no peritonitis, no ischemia, sigmoid volvulus
pathways.treatment: Endoscopic detorsion + rectal tube. Elective sigmoid resection during same admission with primary anastomosis.

pathways.criteria: peritonitis, gangrene, failed endoscopic reduction, cecal volvulus
pathways.treatment: Emergency laparotomy. Resection of non-viable bowel. Hartmann procedure for sigmoid. Right hemicolectomy for cecal. ICU care.

operative_templates.indication: Sigmoid volvulus after successful endoscopic detorsion, viable bowel
steps: Position patient in Lloyd-Davies with Trendelenburg tilt
Access abdomen — open (Hasson) or Veress needle
Diagnostic laparoscopy — inspect bowel viability
Mobilize sigmoid colon — incise peritoneum at white line of Toldt
Identify left ureter and gonadal vessels — preserve
Identify inferior mesenteric artery and divide with ligation
Mobilize descending colon to allow tension-free anastomosis
Divide proximal margin (descending colon) with stapler
Divide distal margin (upper rectum) at sacral promontory
Extract specimen via Pfannenstiel incision or wound protector
Create colorectal anastomosis (EEA stapler) or hand-sewn
Check anastomotic integrity — air leak test
Place drain if indicated
Close fascia and skin

operative_templates.indication: Gangrenous sigmoid volvulus with peritonitis or contamination
operative_templates.incision: Midline laparotomy from xiphisternum to pubis
steps: Midline laparotomy, extended as needed
Four-quadrant abdominal exploration
Identify gangrenous sigmoid loop — note extent of ischemia
Deliver sigmoid colon into wound
Divide mesentery of sigmoid with ligation of vessels
Divide colon proximally (descending colon) — viable margin
Divide rectum distally at peritoneal reflection
Oversew or staple rectal stump — leave closed
Bring descending colon out as end colostomy (left iliac fossa)
Copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline
Place pelvic drain
Close laparotomy with tension-reducing technique
Apply colostomy bag

operative_templates.indication: Cecal volvulus with non-viable cecum or failed cecopexy
steps: Midline laparotomy, assess bowel viability
Mobilize right colon — white line of Toldt incision
Identify right ureter and duodenum — protect
Divide ileocolic, right colic, and right branch of middle colic vessels
Transect terminal ileum (10-15cm from ileocecal valve)
Transect transverse colon
Remove right colon and specimen
Create ileocolic anastomosis (side-to-side or end-to-side)
Close mesenteric defect
Place drain, close abdomen

dischargeCriteria: No fever or signs of sepsis

patient_education.explanation: Volvulus is a twisting of the large bowel that causes a blockage. In Kenya, the high-fiber diet from ugali, githeri, and other cereals can lead to an elongated colon that predisposes to twisting. If not treated promptly, the twisted bowel can lose its blood supply and die, requiring emergency surgery. After successful treatment, you will need an operation to remove the redundant segment to prevent recurrence.
patient_education.warningSigns: Return to hospital immediately if you experience: severe abdominal pain that does not go away, increasing abdominal swelling, inability to pass stool or gas, vomiting (especially if feculent/brown), fever with chills, or wound redness and discharge if you have had surgery.
patient_education.activity: Avoid heavy lifting and straining for 6 weeks after surgery. Gentle walking is encouraged from day 1 to prevent blood clots. Return to normal activities gradually. If your occupation involves heavy labour (farming, construction, boda-boda riding), discuss a gradual return with your surgeon.
patient_education.diet: After surgery, start with clear fluids and advance to soft diet as tolerated. Once fully recovered, maintain a moderate fiber diet — avoid excessive amounts of high-fiber foods like large portions of ugali and githeri at one sitting. Chew food well. Drink at least 6-8 glasses of water daily. Avoid constipation with gentle laxatives if needed.
patient_education.medication: Take all prescribed antibiotics and pain medications as directed. Use stool softeners (Lactulose) to prevent constipation. If you have a stoma, you will receive stoma care supplies and training. Do not take traditional herbal remedies without consulting your doctor.
patient_education.followUp: Return to surgical outpatient clinic in 1 week for wound check, then at 6 weeks to discuss definitive surgery if endoscopic detorsion was performed. Stoma patients will be seen by the stoma therapist. Colonoscopy may be arranged 6 months after recovery to exclude underlying pathology.

followup.before_discharge: Ensure patient passing flatus/stool. Wound inspection. Review blood work (electrolytes, lactate trending down). Patient and family educated on warning signs. Stoma care teaching if applicable. Oral intake tolerance confirmed.

after_discharge.1_week: Wound check and suture removal if non-absorbable. Review histology results. Assess stoma function if applicable. Check for any signs of wound infection or anastomotic leak.
after_discharge.2_weeks: Clinical review — ensure normal bowel function. Discuss elective sigmoid resection date if endoscopic detorsion was done. Nutritional assessment and counseling.
after_discharge.6_weeks: Full recovery assessment. Plan for definitive sigmoid resection if not yet done. Stoma reversal discussion if applicable. Return to work evaluation — provide medical certificate if needed.
after_discharge.3_months: Final follow-up after definitive surgery. Colonoscopy if indicated (age >50 or family history of CRC). Incisional hernia surveillance. Discharge from surgical care if asymptomatic.

followup.when_to_return: Immediately if: severe abdominal pain, vomiting, abdominal distension, fever, inability to pass stool/gas for >24h, wound redness or discharge, bleeding per rectum, or any signs of dehydration (dry mouth, dark urine, dizziness).
